noveltyJustification: Three new findings (F-022 RemoveProject unsafe under concurrent Index, F-023 _ADAPTERS no close path with measured per-session accumulation rate, F-024 sidecar fallback-pin hazard) plus closure of two iter-004 tail questions and a five-packet remediation synthesis ready for Codex-lane refinement.
summary: RemoveProjectRequest cannot be repurposed as a cancel path: it deletes registry pointers under an active indexer with no task cancel, opening KeyError + double-indexer + sqlite-vec-closed-under-write races. _ADAPTERS accumulates ≤4 heavy adapter entries per session with zero close path; HttpSidecarRerankerAdapter additionally pins an httpx.Client pool and (on first 5xx) a bundled CrossEncoder fallback inside the same daemon, neutering sidecar memory savings.

title: RemoveProjectRequest mid-IndexRequest does NOT cancel the in-flight index; it pops registry pointers and calls project.close() under an active _run_index task, opening KeyError + double-indexer + sqlite-vec-closed-under-write races

_ADAPTERS module-global cache has no close path and no bounded eviction; per-session accumulation is bounded but deterministic (1-4 heavy entries, ~1.5-12 GB resident), invisible to ProjectRegistry.close_all and to Project.close, releaseable only by daemon restart

HttpSidecarRerankerAdapter pins both an httpx.Client TCP pool and (on first 5xx) a bundled CrossEncoderRerankerAdapter fallback inside the same daemon, with no close path; sidecar mode does not reduce daemon-side memory pressure once any transient failure triggers the fallback

note: Handler concurrency model confirmed: daemon.py:1237-1255 spawns one asyncio.Task per inbound Connection via _spawn_handler. IndexRequest and RemoveProjectRequest for the same project_root therefore run on co-resident tasks with no serialization. dispatch_request (daemon.py:1000-1072) does not consult _index_locks before invoking remove_project, and remove_project itself (daemon.py:816-830) holds no lock and awaits nothing.

_run_index orphan path: when remove_project pops _index_locks[project_root] mid-flight, the in-flight _run_index task still holds a strong reference to the lock object captured at line 458, so its lock.release() at line 473 still works. However the daemon shutdown gather at daemon.py:1244+ only tracks the OUTER handler task, not the inner index_task spawned at daemon.py:630. Orphan _run_index tasks accumulate invisible to shutdown bookkeeping.

Re-add race: after remove_project pops the entries, a subsequent IndexRequest or SearchRequest for the same project_root calls get_project (daemon.py:358-379), which re-creates a fresh Project, fresh _index_locks[project_root] asyncio.Lock(), and fresh _load_time_done event. The orphan _run_index holds the OLD lock; the new code path holds the NEW lock. Two concurrent indexers write to the same .cocoindex_code/target_sqlite.db. Per-project-lock guarantee is broken; silent sqlite-vec corruption is the worst observable outcome.

_ADAPTERS keying conventions: (a) f'__http_sidecar__:{model_name}' when COCOINDEX_RERANK_VIA_SIDECAR is on (default true per _rerank_via_sidecar_enabled at reranker.py:24-35); (b) bare model_name when sidecar gate is off. Toggling COCOINDEX_RERANK_VIA_SIDECAR mid-session accumulates BOTH keys for the same logical model. Cross-section with COCOINDEX_RERANK_FALLBACK_MODEL doubles again on fallback path.

Tree-wide grep confirms zero production close callers for _ADAPTERS entries or for httpx.Client instances. Only test fixtures reset _ADAPTERS via monkeypatch.setattr (tests/test_rerank_dispatch.py, tests/test_reranker.py, tests/test_http_sidecar_adapter.py). No close_all_adapters(), no atexit hook, no signal hook, no __del__, no weakref.finalize. The cache is process-lifetime-bound.

Per-session accumulation rate summary (deferred iter-003 audit, closed): fixed-config session = 1 entry; with fallback = 2; with sidecar toggle = 2; bench-lane swap (016/007/003) = up to 3; plus HttpSidecarRerankerAdapter._fallback_adapter hidden fallback adds 1 more model footprint on first 5xx. Worst realistic case ~4-7 loaded reranker model footprints per long-lived daemon, ~1.5-12 GB resident.

Five-packet remediation synthesis ready for Codex lane (iter-006+): (1) daemon-protocol-cancel-index-surface [F-019/F-020 + F-021 subsumption]; (2) mcp-bg-index-task-lifecycle [F-005/F-010/F-017/F-018, prereq packet 1]; (3) remove-project-cancel-safety [F-022, new]; (4) adapter-lifecycle-management [F-023/F-024, new]; (5) project-close-deep-vs-shallow + embedder-cache-eviction-on-close [carry-over from iter-002 F-011/F-012/F-014/F-016]. Packets 1-4 are independently shippable; packet 3 becomes packet 1's daemon-side cancel building block.

title: F-022 (remove-project safety) is a prerequisite for the F-019/F-020 cancel-surface packet: the daemon-side CancelIndexRequest handler will need a _cancel_in_flight_index primitive that does task lookup + cancel + bounded await + project.close(); F-022's fix delivers exactly that primitive and lets RemoveProject reuse it

hypothesis: RemoveProjectRequest as-is can be repurposed as a narrow per-project cancel primitive while a full CancelIndexRequest is being designed
reason: Static read of daemon.py:1052-1054 + 816-830 + 443-473 + 610-660 shows three concurrent-task race modes: (a) _run_index KeyError on self._projects[project_root] if pop wins the scheduling race; (b) sqlite-vec ManagedConnection closed under active project.update_index() write; (c) re-add race produces two concurrent _run_index tasks on the same target DB after the next IndexRequest/SearchRequest. Existing primitive is itself a latent correctness bug; cannot be load-bearing for cancellation.

hypothesis: _ADAPTERS accumulates unboundedly per request in normal operation
reason: Static read of rerankers/reranker.py:385-414 shows cache keys are bounded by (model_name × sidecar_gate × fallback_model). All three are env-fixed at daemon start in production callers (no per-request model_name override outside tests). Cache stops growing after ≤4 entries per daemon. Growth is deterministic and fixed-cost, not runaway; severity P2 (resident-memory bias), not P1 (runaway leak).

hypothesis: Sidecar mode reduces daemon-side rerank memory pressure as designed
reason: rerankers/reranker.py:259-262 + 263-279 show HttpSidecarRerankerAdapter lazy-creates a bundled CrossEncoderRerankerAdapter as _fallback_adapter on first HTTP failure (sidecar_unavailable / sidecar_5xx / sidecar_4xx / sidecar_malformed). After a single transient sidecar failure in a long-lived daemon, the bundled fallback model is loaded and pinned for the rest of the daemon's life — exactly the cost sidecar mode was supposed to delegate away.

hypothesis: Test-fixture monkeypatch.setattr(reranker_module, '_ADAPTERS', {}) is an existing close path that could be exposed to production
reason: The fixture overwrites the module attribute with a fresh empty dict. It drops references but does NOT invoke close() on any of the cleared adapters — there is no close() to invoke. Exposing it without first adding close() to the adapter classes would leak httpx.Client TCP pools and torch CrossEncoder tensors. The fixture is correct for tests (where the process exits immediately after) but unsafe as a production primitive.
